step_by_step_reasoning: First, we match the entities from the World Model to create 8 nodes (Mobile App, Route 53, ELB, EC2, EKS, SPOT, RDS, S3). SPOT and S3 are drawn without arrows directly attached in the diagram. Next, we build the connections as per the World Model: Mobile App resolves domain via Route 53 (flow 0, seq 0), then sends application traffic to ELB (flow 0, seq 1). ELB routes traffic to Nomad/EC2 (flow 0, seq 2) and EKS (flow 0, seq 3). The migration pathway is shown from EC2 to EKS (flow 1, seq 0). Finally, microservices in both EC2 (flow 2, seq 0) and EKS (flow 2, seq 1) persist their data to RDS.

graph.name: Cabify Platform Migration from Nomad to Amazon EKS
graph.notes: Cabify migrates 700+ microservices from a legacy Nomad platform hosted on EC2 to a managed Amazon EKS cluster, leveraging Spot instances for cost resiliency and RDS MySQL databases per service.

nodes.name: Mobile App
nodes.notes: Represents the mobile application used by riders and drivers to access Cabify's platform.

nodes.name: ROUTE53
nodes.notes: Used for DNS resolution and geolocalized routing during country-by-country migration.

nodes.name: ELB
nodes.notes: Distributes incoming client application traffic to backend services.

nodes.name: EC2
nodes.notes: Hosts the legacy Nomad container platform representing over 700 services.

nodes.name: EKS
nodes.notes: The new target managed Kubernetes cluster to which services are being migrated.

nodes.name: SPOT
nodes.notes: Used to cost-effectively run services and test resiliency against instance terminations.

nodes.name: RDS
nodes.notes: Hosts independent MySQL database instances for service persistence.

nodes.name: S3
nodes.notes: Used for long-term cold data archiving of trip information.

edges.notes: App resolves domain using Route 53 IP geolocalisation.
App traffic goes to the load balancers.
Traffic distributed to services running on Nomad on EC2.
Traffic distributed to services running on EKS.
Visual representation of the migration process from Nomad (EC2) to EKS.
Nomad-hosted services writing persistent data to RDS databases.
EKS-hosted services writing persistent data to RDS databases.
